edge: Semantic segmentation models aim to segment images into different classes or categories using a specific model architecture.
The study focuses on semantic segmentation under the domain generalization setting, which aims to improve model robustness against domain differences.
Existing works show that Vision Transformers are more robust than CNNs in semantic segmentation tasks.
Vision Transformers show better performance due to their visual grouping property of self-attention.
HGFormer explicitly groups pixels to form part-level masks and whole-level masks for semantic segmentation tasks.
HGFormer combines mask classification results at different scales for improved class label prediction.
HGFormer is evaluated using seven public semantic segmentation datasets to show its robustness and outperformance over previous methods.
Images are segmented into classification units for the purpose of classification.
Mask classification is considered more robust than per-pixel classification because masks allow aggregation of features over large image regions.
Pixels are grouped into part-level masks first, then part-level masks are grouped into whole-level masks for reliable segmentation.
Part-level and whole-level mask classification are combined for robust semantic segmentation as they capture complementary information.
Deep neural networks have significantly improved research in semantic image segmentation.
Distribution shift, such as Gaussian Noise, is a challenge when grouping pixels into masks.
In domain generalization setting, models are trained on source domains and tested on target domains.
Target data is not accessible/needed during training in domain adaptation, making the task challenging but practically useful.
Vision Transformers have been shown to be significantly more robust than traditional CNNs in out-of-distribution generalization.
Some works interpret self-attention as a kind of visual grouping and believe that it is related to robustness.
FAN and Segformer have been evaluated on segmentation but do not explicitly introduce visual grouping in their networks.
Most deep learning based segmentation models directly conduct per-pixel classification without the process of grouping.
Some recent segmentation models introduced flat grouping in the segmentation decoder, where pixels are grouped into a set of binary masks directly.
DETR uses a one-to-one matching similar to DETR, where the loss between predicted masks and ground truth masks is computed.
DETR and one-to-one matching are similar concepts in the context of loss computation between predicted masks and ground truth masks.
predicted masks and ground truth masks are the entities used to compute loss in the context.
whole-level masks directly predict mask classification, leading to more robust results due to information aggregation.
flat grouping is susceptible to errors when generating whole-level masks, especially under cross-domain settings.
hierarchical grouping is proposed in the segmentation decoder, where pixels are grouped into part-level masks first, and then into whole-level masks.
part-level masks are grouped first, followed by grouping into whole-level masks, showing a hierarchical segmentation approach.
hierarchical grouping in the context is inspired by pioneer works in image segmentation and psychological evidence related to scene parsing.
part-level masks and whole-level masks complement each other by segmenting images at different scales and improving overall robustness.

Classiﬁcation in the domain generalization context has been widely studied in previous works.
Some previous methods focused on domain generalization (DG) in semantic segmentation, including Randomization and Normalization.
Vision Transformers have demonstrated robustness in the out-of-distribution setting, according to literature [70].
The robustness of Vision Transformer has been attributed to the grouping property of self-attention.
There are no works studying the effect of explicit grouping in the segmentation decoder for semantic segmentation in domain generalization (DG).
The study involves exploring different levels of grouping and mask classification for semantic segmentation in the context of domain generalization (DG).
Semantic segmentation involves mapping each region to a class, thereby defining the segmentation of the image into regions.
Region mapping assigns each region to a specific class, forming a set of classes.
The pixel partition is used in per-pixel classification methods to classify each pixel individually.
The pixel partition is distinct from the whole-level masks partition, which represents entire masks of a class.
Recent approaches use the whole-level masks partition to generate masks for different classes directly from pixels.
The part-level mask partition works alongside the whole-level masks partition to enhance robustness in segmentation by focusing on covering class parts.
Mask classification is considered more robust than per-pixel classification as it allows for aggregating features over large image regions of the same class.
Generating whole-level masks directly from pixels is a challenging task, despite leading methods being able to produce reasonable results.
SOTA methods can generate whole-level masks directly from pixels.
flat grouping methods are not robust to domain changes, resulting in poor quality generated masks.
Poor quality generated masks lead to low semantic segmentation performance.
Hierarchical grouping is proposed in the segmentation transformer architecture to group pixels to part-level masks first and then group part-level masks to whole-level masks.
The grouping task becomes less challenging and mask classification can be performed at both scales.
Mask classification at both scales can be combined for more robust label prediction.
Masks at two scales capture complementary information.
Global self-attention is used to aggregate long-range context information directly at both part-level and whole-level.
The model can predict semantic segmentation at different scales by adopting a hierarchical grouping process.
The hierarchical grouping process consists of two stages: grouping pixels into part-level masks and grouping part-level masks into whole-level masks.
Classification is done on partitions at different scales in the model framework.
Part-level grouping is the process of computing a partition Sm = {R1, ..., RNp} to assign pixels to different masks

Semantic segmentation results O2 can be obtained by combining whole-level masks and their classiﬁcations.
The final results O are the sum of semantic segmentation results O1 and O2.

The assignment matrix is used to extract part-level tokens from feature map V by calculating Z = A × V.
Z is passed to a self-attention layer to strengthen the part-level mask features.
Z is also passed to a feed forward network (FFN) layer to strengthen the part-level mask features.
Z is processed to get Z' after passing through the self-attention and FFN layers.
Z' is further processed using a linear classifier layer for part-level class predictions.
Z' is then used with a softmax activation layer to map to part-level class predictions.
Transformer decoders are used to group part-level masks into whole-level masks.

The final semantic segmentation result is an ensemble of two results by a simple addition.
